html:
[424]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昏乱中见一物如猫 - Literally 'in a confused state, saw something like a cat,' describing a hallucination or vision.
  • 头绾双髻 - Describes the hairstyle of the small boy, literally 'tied up in two buns.'
  • 辄寒热交作 - Means 'immediately alternating between chills and fever,' a symptom of malaria.
  • 至昏昏睡去 - Means 'until falling into a deep sleep,' indicating the effect of the malaria.
  • 悟其为疟鬼 - Means 'realized it was a malaria demon,' identifying the cause of the symptoms.
  • 欲驱之，而无术也 - Means 'wanted to drive it away, but had no method,' expressing the helplessness of the woman.
  • 作退缩状 - Means 'made a retreating gesture,' describing a reaction to the presence of the object.
  • 窗上有剖瓜刀一柄 - Literally 'a knife for slicing melons was on the window,' indicating the object that the woman used to scare the demon.
  • 吱吱嗥叫而遁 - Describes the sound the demon made as it fled, literally 'squeaking and howling and fleeing.'
  • 营造墓田极奢侈 - Means 'constructed a cemetery plot extremely lavishly,' describing the opulence of the construction.
  • 旁建房舍亦宏壮 - Means 'built houses nearby that were also grand,' indicating the scale of the construction.
  • 怪而察之 - Means 'found it strange and investigated it,' describing the reaction of the wealthy family.
  • 殊无盗迹 - Means 'there were no signs of theft,' indicating the absence of evidence of theft.
  • 疑仆，诮责之 - Means 'suspected the servant and scolded him,' describing the reaction of the wealthy family.
  • 仆冤甚 - Means 'the servant was very aggrieved,' indicating the servant's distress.
  • 夜伺之 - Means 'waited for it at night,' describing the servant's vigilance.
  • 见二巨人头大如釜，身高丈余 - Describes the appearance of the giants, literally 'saw two giants with heads as large as cauldrons and heights over ten feet.'
  • 月光下金铠辉煌 - Describes the appearance of the giants, literally 'in the moonlight, their golden armor shone brilliantly.'
  • 手持画戟 - Describes the weapons of the giants, literally 'holding painted halberds.'
  • 越垣过 - Means 'jumped over the wall,' describing the action of the giants.
  • 直至仓房 - Means 'until they reached the granary,' indicating the destination of the giants.
  • 门自启，鞠躬入 - Describes the entry of the giants, literally 'the door opened itself, and they bowed and entered.'
  • 俯掬菽麦 - Describes the action of the giants, literally 'bent down and scooped up beans and wheat.'
  • 饱饫而去 - Means 'satisfied and left,' indicating the completion of their action.
  • 仆遥尾之 - Means 'the servant followed them from a distance,' describing the servant's actions.
  • 入梁氏殡宫 - Means 'entered the funeral hall of the Li family,' indicating the location of the giants.
  • 破其腹，得菽麦数十斛 - Describes the discovery of the source of the theft, literally 'opened their stomachs and found dozens of shi of beans and wheat.'
  • 火之，怪遂绝 - Means 'burned them, and the strange occurrences ceased,' indicating the resolution of the problem.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 疟疾 - Refers to malaria, a disease prevalent in certain regions of China.
  • 疟鬼 - Refers to the demon associated with malaria, a common belief in traditional Chinese medicine and folklore.
  • 剖瓜刀 - Refers to a knife used for slicing melons, a common household tool.
  • 刍灵 - Refers to straw figures used in funerals, often to represent the deceased.
  • 方弼、方相 - Refers to specific straw figures used in funeral rituals, often associated with protective or guiding roles.
  • 金铠辉煌 - Describes the appearance of the giants, reflecting the traditional imagery of demons and spirits in Chinese folklore.
  • 画戟 - Refers to a type of weapon, often associated with warriors and demons in Chinese mythology.

Proper Nouns

  • 苏州 - Refers to Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, China.
  • 李氏妇 - Refers to the woman of the Li family.
  • 津 - Refers to Tianjin, a city in Hebei Province, China.
  • 梁氏 - Refers to the Li family, the owners of the cemetery plot.
  • 方弼、方相 - Refers to specific straw figures used in funeral rituals.

[425]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 缸怪 - A term combining '缸' (jar or vat) and '怪' (strange or supernatural), likely referring to a supernatural phenomenon related to a jar or vat.
  • 投缸毙者 - Literally 'those who die by being thrown into a jar,' a phrase that describes a mysterious and fatal event.
  • 碌碡 - A traditional farming tool used for threshing grain, often rolled over crops to separate the grain from the chaff. Here, it is used metaphorically to describe a rolling sound.
  • 倒矗 - Literally 'upright and inverted,' describing a person standing upside down inside a jar.
  • 竖其双足 - Literally 'standing on his feet,' describing the person's position inside the jar.
  • 旋转不休 - Literally 'continuously rotating,' describing the movement of the person inside the jar.
  • 犬吠随之 - Literally 'dogs bark following,' indicating that the dogs' barking follows the strange event.
  • 人见者辄病 - Literally 'those who see it fall ill,' indicating that people who witness the event become sick.
  • 宅几易数主 - Literally 'the house has changed hands several times,' indicating that the house has been sold multiple times due to the strange occurrences.
  • 居皆不安 - Literally 'all residents are uneasy,' indicating that the residents of the house are constantly worried.
  • 拆房重修 - Literally 'demolish and rebuild the house,' indicating that the house was torn down and rebuilt.
  • 术匣 - Literally 'magical box,' a container that holds something magical or supernatural.
  • 小泥缸 - Literally 'small clay jar,' a miniature version of a jar or vat.
  • 倒竖其中 - Literally 'upright and inverted inside,' describing the position of the small clay jar inside the magical box.
  • 工师之妖术 - Literally 'the witchcraft of the craftsman,' indicating that the strange events are caused by the craftsman's supernatural abilities.
  • 邪祟 - Literally 'evil spirits,' referring to supernatural beings that cause harm or misfortune.
  • 工师镇魇之术 - Literally 'the craftsman's binding and subduing technique,' indicating a method used by the craftsman to control or bind supernatural entities.
  • 致使人投缸而毙 - Literally 'causing people to die by being thrown into a jar,' indicating the harmful effects of the craftsman's techniques.
  • 邪术亦可畏也已 - Literally 'even evil magic is to be feared,' emphasizing the danger of the craftsman's supernatural abilities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 工师 - Refers to a skilled craftsman or artisan, often associated with traditional Chinese craftsmanship.
  • 镇魇之术 - A traditional Chinese practice involving rituals or techniques to bind and subdue evil spirits or supernatural entities.
  • 邪术 - Refers to black magic or sorcery, often associated with harmful or malevolent practices.
  • 醉茶子 - A section attributed to 'Drunk Tea,' possibly a pseudonym or a character in the text, providing commentary or moral lessons.

Proper Nouns

  • 缸怪 - A term combining '缸' (jar or vat) and '怪' (strange or supernatural), likely referring to a supernatural phenomenon related to a jar or vat.
  • 醉茶子 - A section attributed to 'Drunk Tea,' possibly a pseudonym or a character in the text, providing commentary or moral lessons.
  • 工师 - Refers to a skilled craftsman or artisan, often associated with traditional Chinese craftsmanship.
  • 镇魇之术 - A traditional Chinese practice involving rituals or techniques to bind and subdue evil spirits or supernatural entities.
  • 邪术 - Refers to black magic or sorcery, often associated with harmful or malevolent practices.
